one_line_takeaway: The paper formalizes and empirically demonstrates that insiders with legitimate access to real data or the synthetic-data-generation pipeline (data owner, outsourced provider, or system intruder) can cheaply degrade synthetic-data quality -- e.g., replacing the real source with mismatched data cut MLP downstream accuracy by 55.53% and raised KLD to 5747.71 -- exposing an integrity gap in privacy-preserving synthetic-data-sharing pipelines that existing receiver-side privacy-attack literature does not address.
key_finding: All tested attack primitives degrade both distributional fidelity and downstream utility, but severity and detectability vary sharply by attack surface. Real-data poisoning (Table 2): FIA (averaged over r=0.1/0.3/0.5) increased WD by 1337.55% and reduced MLP accuracy by 11.57% (also LR -9.56%, RF -13.21%; KS +743.70%, KLD +3479.04%); LFA increased WD by 322.34% and reduced MLP accuracy by 17.08% (LR -15.52%, RF -18.37%; KS +392.27%, KLD +2458.63%). The tampering-ratio effect is opposite between the two attacks: for LFA, higher r is a stronger attack (MLP drop worsens from -2.87% at r=0.1 to -44.03% at r=0.5); for FIA, lower r is stronger because r denotes the fraction of features retained (r=0.1, i.e., most extreme suppression, gives WD=2926.74% and MLP -8.89%, the largest divergence increase in the FIA row). Broader attack-surface comparison (Table 3, averaged across models/datasets): 'Oversimplified SDG' (GMM) and 'Low epochs' cause only modest degradation (Oversimplified SDG: LR -2.10%, MLP -1.36%, WD +7.06; Low epochs: LR -1.12%, MLP -1.61%, WD +99.60) -- the paper notes these are 'less destructive but also harder to detect via large divergence signals.' In contrast, source-level and post-generation attacks are far more destructive: 'Incorrect/inappropriate data source' produced WD=1335.48, KLD=5747.71, and MLP accuracy drop of -55.53% (the single largest utility drop in the paper); 'Noise injection' produced WD=1079.37 with a large LR drop of -41.33% but comparatively small RF (-0.82%) and MLP (-2.17%) drops, illustrating heterogeneous classifier sensitivity; 'Distribution distortion' (scaling) produced WD=856.31, KLD=4588.07, LR -18.17%. Averaged across all five attack types: WD +475.36%, KLD +2411.90%, LR -17.30%, RF -2.68%, MLP -12.86%. The paper's stated overall conclusion is that divergence does not uniformly predict utility loss (classifiers respond heterogeneously to the same distributional shift), so both statistical-fidelity and predictive-utility metrics are needed to characterize poisoning impact.
core_contribution: The paper's own novel contribution (not related work) is: (1) a formal threat model for 'quality degradation attacks' on synthetic data generation (SDG) pipelines used for privacy-preserving data sharing, where the adversary is an insider who already has legitimate or illegitimate access to the real data -- the data owner ('Client A'), an outsourced synthetic-data provider ('Provider B'), or a third party who penetrated either system -- rather than the traditionally studied adversary who is the *recipient* of synthetic data trying to infer private information from it; and (2) an empirical study, across three tabular datasets (Adult, Law, Loan) and three SDG models (CTGAN, TVAE, NFLOW), of five concrete attack primitives spanning upstream, in-processing, and post-generation stages of the SDG pipeline: label-flipping (LFA), feature-importance-based feature suppression (FIA), substituting an incorrect/mismatched data source, using an oversimplified generator (Gaussian Mixture Model) or too few training epochs, and post-generation output tampering (constant-factor scaling / Gaussian noise injection). The authors state explicitly that classic poisoning primitives (LFA/FIA) have been studied in standard ML but 'prior work has not explored how classic poisoning primitives translate to synthetic data generation,' and their paper 'provide[s] the first systematic examination of this connection' for SDG. Privacy attacks (membership inference, attribute inference, reconstruction/re-identification attacks against synthetic data) discussed in Section 2.2 are explicitly attributed to prior work (Stadler et al. USENIX Security'22, Annamalai et al. USENIX Security'24, Ganev & De Cristofaro, Andrey et al., Alshantti et al.) and are NOT part of this paper's contribution -- they are cited only to establish that all prior synthetic-data attack literature assumes a receiver-side adversary, which this paper's threat model breaks from.
threat_model: Adversary types: (i) Client A, the real-data owner, who has direct access to the real dataset D and controls what is fed into or how the SDG process is configured; (ii) Provider B, an outsourced synthetic-data-generation company that is legally granted access to D for the purpose of generation; (iii) a third party who gains indirect access to D or the SDG process via system penetration of either Client A or Provider B. The attacker can modify the real input data, SDG training parameters/configuration, or post-generation outputs. The attacker does NOT know Receiver C's exact verification mechanism, is aware Receiver C has no direct access to the real dataset D (so detection is inherently hard because Receiver C 'cannot reliably audit the synthetic data'), and cannot control Receiver C's downstream tasks. The formalized attacker objective is to maximize distributional divergence D(p_D~, p_D) between synthetic and real distributions subject to a tampering-ratio budget |D_a|/|D| <= r, with r in {10%, 30%, 50%} tested experimentally. The paper explicitly scopes privacy leakage as out-of-scope ('Privacy evaluation is out of scope, as our threat model assumes the attacker is the data owner or provider who does not aim to leak information') -- the goal is availability/integrity degradation of synthetic-data utility and fidelity, not confidentiality attack.
limitations: The authors explicitly state their own limitation: 'Our attacks are intentionally simple and targeted at tabular data. Extending the framework to other modalities (e.g., image backdoors or adversarial perturbations) remains an important direction for broader applicability.' Additional limitations evident from the text (not necessarily flagged by the authors): the attack objective is defined purely as maximizing distributional divergence rather than directly optimizing downstream-task degradation, justified by an assumption (backed only by a single cited reference, [7]) that greater distribution gap implies worse downstream performance -- yet the paper's own Table 3 results (e.g., noise injection producing a large LR drop but small RF/MLP drops despite large WD) show this relationship is not uniform, somewhat undercutting the simplifying assumption used to define the attacker's objective. No defenses, detection mechanisms, or mitigation techniques are proposed or evaluated -- the paper only recommends (as future work / discussion) 'integrity verification and robustness mechanisms' such as provenance tracking, auditing SDG configurations, or post-hoc consistency checks, without implementing or testing any of them. Only three tabular datasets and three generator architectures were tested. Privacy leakage is explicitly excluded from the evaluation, so the paper cannot say whether these same manipulations also affect privacy risk.

datasets_used: Adult (48k rows, target 'income>50K', 8 continuous/1 binary/4 multi-class categorical features)
Law (50k rows, target 'first_pf', 8 continuous/2 binary/4 multi-class categorical features)
Loan (21k rows, target 'Personal Loan', 4 continuous/5 binary/1 multi-class categorical feature, imbalance ratio 8)
quantitative_results: LFA at r=0.5: MLP -44.03% (vs -2.87% at r=0.1), showing tampering ratio scales attack strength for LFA
FIA at r=0.1 (most extreme feature suppression): WD +2926.74%, MLP -8.89% (largest WD increase in the FIA row)
Oversimplified SDG (GMM, 10 components): LR -2.10%, RF -1.21%, MLP -1.36%, WD +7.06, KS +277.33, KLD +404.84
Incorrect/inappropriate data source: LR -23.79%, RF -8.59%, MLP -55.53%, WD 1335.48, KS 675.55, KLD 5747.71
Distribution distortion (scale factor s=2): LR -18.17%, RF -1.17%, MLP -1.64%, WD 856.31, KS 418.95, KLD 4588.07
Overall average across five attack types: LR -17.30%, RF -2.68%, MLP -12.86%, WD 475.36, KS 377.90, KLD 2411.90
baselines_compared: Clean (unattacked) SDG pipeline as the reference baseline for all percentage-change computations
Three SDG generator architectures compared under the same attacks: CTGAN, TVAE, NFLOW (results in Tables 2-3 are averaged across all three)
Three downstream classifiers used to measure TSTR utility degradation: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, MLP
